title: The Beast of Gévaudan: Modern Theories 📁
segments: Between June 1764 and June 1767, in the rural Gévaudan region of southern France, a creature attacked and killed at least 100 people. Most victims were young shepherds and peasant women. The Beast of Gévaudan terrified the kingdom of Louis XV. Modern researchers still debate what the beast actually was.

The first attack happened on June 30, 1764. A 14-year-old shepherdess named Jeanne Boulet was killed near the village of Saint-Étienne-de-Lugdarès. Witnesses described an animal larger than a wolf, with reddish fur and a striped back.

Within weeks, attacks spread across Gévaudan. Most victims were children or teenagers tending livestock alone. Many had their throats torn out. Some had heads removed. Witnesses repeatedly described an unusually large animal that struck quickly and ate parts of its victims.

By the autumn of 1764, the panic in Gévaudan had reached Versailles. King Louis XV ordered the dragoons under Captain Jean-Baptiste Boulanger Duhamel to hunt the beast. Duhamel led several months of organized hunts. He killed many wolves but the attacks continued.

In February 1765, the king sent his chief huntsman, Jean Charles Marc Antoine Vaumesle d'Enneval, with bloodhounds and a trained hunting party. They spent six months in the field. They killed wolves and other predators. The beast continued to attack.
By June 1765 the king had replaced d'Enneval with François Antoine de Beauterne, his personal arquebusier. Antoine was given a substantial budget and authority over local militias.

Here's where the official version of the case ends. On September 21, 1765, Antoine shot a large wolf at the Royal abbey of Chazes. The wolf was over five feet long and weighed about 60 kilograms. Antoine had it stuffed and presented it at Versailles. The king awarded him 9,400 livres. The Beast of Gévaudan was officially dead.
The attacks did not stop. Children and women continued to be killed in Gévaudan throughout 1765, 1766, and into 1767. The royal court, having declared the beast dead, did not act on the new attacks.

On June 19, 1767, a local hunter named Jean Chastel shot another large animal during a coordinated hunt at Mont Mouchet. Local accounts describe Chastel killing the animal with a silver bullet — though that detail is largely folklore. The carcass was paraded through Saulgues and Marvejols. After Chastel's kill, the attacks stopped.

What was the Beast of Gévaudan? Modern researchers have proposed multiple theories. The conventional position is that the killings were committed by one or more unusually large wolves, possibly a male and female pair, with their offspring continuing the pattern.

Some researchers have proposed that the beast was a wolf-dog hybrid. Others have suggested an escaped exotic animal — a hyena from a noble's menagerie or a lion brought back from Africa. There is no documented evidence supporting either claim.

A more controversial theory was published by historian Karl-Hans Taake in 2015. Taake examined witness descriptions, victim wound patterns, and contemporary illustrations. He argued that the beast was likely a young lion that had escaped from a private menagerie or a traveling exhibition.
Taake's argument relies on the descriptions of stripes on the animal's back, the distinctive face, and the size of the wounds — all of which match a lion more closely than a wolf in his analysis.

Other modern researchers have argued for a serial killer using trained dogs as a smokescreen. Eyewitness accounts of a man near some attack scenes have fueled this hypothesis. The most prominent advocate has been French zoologist Michel Louis, whose 2005 book argued for an organized human predator using a dog trained to attack.

Some witness statements from the 1760s do mention a man seen near attack sites. Most peasants at the time would not have considered organized human predation as the explanation. The wolf was the obvious culprit in their experience.

Modern wildlife biologists studying wolf behavior generally consider single-wolf killing of 100 humans implausible without exceptional circumstances — disease, severe injury, or human conditioning. A wolf with rabies or with prior exposure to human food sources could potentially attack repeatedly, but the geographic spread of the Gévaudan attacks is unusual.

Bone analysis on what is believed to be the carcass killed by Chastel — preserved at Le Conservatoire National des Arts et Métiers and studied in 2015 — produced inconclusive results. The animal showed canid morphology but with unusual size and proportions.

The official records of the Gévaudan attacks are preserved at the French national archives. Local parish records list the victims by name and date. Historians have catalogued at least 113 confirmed deaths and roughly 49 additional injuries.

Gévaudan today is part of the Lozère department. The villages where the attacks happened still exist. Some maintain small museums and walking tours of the attack sites. The town of Saugues hosts a Beast of Gévaudan museum.

The case has shaped French rural memory deeply. Films, novels, and folklore have built on the original events for over 250 years. The 2001 film 'Le Pacte des Loups' (Brotherhood of the Wolf) drew on the human-conspiracy theory.

Whether the beast was wolf, lion, hybrid, or human-orchestrated, it represents a real and documented period of mass killing in 1760s France. Over 100 actual victims died. Their deaths are recorded in the parish books.

The case is also studied in early modern European history as an example of how monarchical, ecclesiastical, and local authorities responded to a sustained crisis. The royal hunt parties, the religious processions, the local militias — all are documented in the original sources.

If you are researching the Beast of Gévaudan, the Archives Départementales de la Lozère in Mende holds the most complete original documentation. The Bibliothèque Nationale de France in Paris holds the royal court records related to the king's interventions.
Local historians in Gévaudan continue to publish ongoing analysis. The mystery of what exactly killed those shepherds and peasants in the 1760s remains a question without a confirmed answer.
